6. How the counterfactual actually works

The question I’m answering is narrow and concrete. Take each of 314 matches from six major tournaments exactly as it was played. Now append the stoppage time the referee should have added but didn’t, and ask one thing: would at least one more goal plausibly have dropped in? Average that probability over all 314 matches and you get the headline.

Step 1. Measure the live football, then validate against outside numbers. Every match comes from StatsBomb’s hand-coded event data: an analyst watches the video and logs every touch — pass, shot, throw-in, foul, card, sub, out of bounds — timestamped to the second. When the ball is dead, nothing gets logged, so a gap opens in the timestamps. Those gaps give me ball-in-play. I validate against the best available anchors: for 2022, my reconstruction reads 57:40 against Opta’s 58:04 (-24s); for 2018, 56:00 against Opta’s 54:50 (+70s), with FiveThirtyEight’s 55:18 in between (+42s).

The measure turns on one global threshold: how long a silent gap must run before the ball counts as dead. Sweeping it from 12s to 30s, the anchors respond at different slopes (2018 ~2.3× more sensitive), so the residual is tournament-specific, not an offset one setting could remove (full sweep in the appendix). I therefore report ball-in-play with ~±1 minute per match of uncertainty (≈±1.4 in-play points), not a point estimate. Crucially, the headline figure doesn’t depend on getting ball-in-play exactly right: pushing the threshold across that entire 12s–30s range shifts the result by less than 0.1 points (it stays at 23.5%–23.7%).

Step 2. Separate stoppage owed from stoppage played, and calibrate that, too. Stoppage actually played is easy: it’s whistle-to-whistle added time, read straight off the event clock. Stoppage owed is the hard part — I estimate it from the dead time the data can identify (goal celebrations, substitutions, cards, injuries, and the excess of slow restarts over a normal allowance), plus the dead-ball gaps the data independently confirms. The estimate is then calibrated against the one piece of independent ground truth that exists: Nate Silver’s stopwatch measurement of all 32 World Cup 2018 matches, where he recorded, by hand, both the stoppage that should have been added and the stoppage that was. My owed-stoppage estimator tracks his “should-have-been-added” column at r = 0.825, and my independently measured played clock tracks his “actually-played” column at r = 0.992, essentially exact (Figure 1).

Figure 1. Validation against Nate Silver’s independent, hand-measured World Cup 2018 data (32 matches). Left: my owed-stoppage estimate vs his “should-have-been-added” minutes — r = 0.825, average error 2.44 minutes; the two labelled points are the model’s hardest cases. Right: my stoppage-played clock vs his “actually-played” minutes — r = 0.992, near-exact. The dashed line is perfect agreement. The near-exact right panel is what makes the gap in the left panel believable: Nate, with a stopwatch and no access to my pipeline, found the same shortfall I do.

That gap is the engine of the whole piece. Across the 314 matches, owed stoppage averages about 17 minutes and played stoppage about 9, leaving roughly 8.6 omitted minutes per match — positive in 97% of matches — of which about 5.1 are live ball-in-play once dead time is stripped out. Nate found the same pattern by hand in 2018: roughly half the owed stoppage simply never got played.

What the rulebook actually requires — and where it goes quiet. The Laws of the Game (Law 7) tell the referee to add on, at the end of each half, all the time lost to substitutions, injuries, goal celebrations, disciplinary action, VAR checks, and deliberate time-wasting. What the Laws never do is put a number on any of it. There is no stated allowance for how long a routine dead ball — a throw-in, a goal kick, a free kick — is permitted to take before the delay counts as time lost; whether a given restart was dragged out in excess is left entirely to the referee’s discretion. So to measure owed stoppage at all, you have to supply the thresholds the rulebook omits.

Nate Silver’s 2018 stopwatch study supplied exactly that: a fixed allowance for each routine restart, with anything beyond it counted as time lost. I adopt his table unchanged — these allowances are wired directly into the owed-stoppage estimator above, frozen on 2018 and applied to all six tournaments:

Routine restart Normal allowance (excess beyond this counts as owed)
Throw-in 20 s
Goal kick 30 s
Corner kick 45 s
Free kick 60 s

So a throw-in that takes 50 seconds contributes 30 seconds of owed stoppage; one that takes 15 contributes nothing. Genuine stoppages — celebrations, substitutions, cards, injuries — are credited in full and separately; this table only governs the grey area the Laws hand to the referee’s judgment.

Step 3 — Price the missing minutes. Each omitted live minute gets a goal rate. Expected extra goals is just rate times minutes; the chance of at least one extra goal follows from the Poisson formula:

For each match, expected extra goals:
    μ  =  (1st-half goal rate)      × (omitted live 1st-half minutes)
        + (decayed 2nd-half rate)   × (omitted live 2nd-half minutes)

Probability of at least one extra goal:
    P(≥1)  =  1 − e^(−μ)

Headline  =  average of P(≥1) across all 314 matches

The one subtlety — and the subject of the next section — is the rate. Late-game minutes score faster than average, but I do not assume the missing minutes inherit that frantic pace. The second-half rate decays back toward the open-play rate as the hypothetical window grows. Run that over all 314 matches and the answer is 23.6% of matches finishing with a different scoreline (95% confidence interval 20.6%–27.4%). Stated separately and more strictly, the result — who actually wins or draws — flips in 12.1% (CI 10.6%–14.2%). These two numbers mean different things and I keep them apart throughout: a scoreline can change without the winner changing, and 95 of the 314 matches were already decided by two or more goals at 90 minutes, so they can’t flip at all.

7. The obvious objection — and why the number survives it

The first thing anyone with a quantitative bone says is: “you can’t price new minutes at the frantic end-of-game rate — teams wouldn’t keep scoring that fast.” They’re right, and the model is built around agreeing with them.

Start by conceding the premise, because it’s true. Goals don’t arrive evenly: scoring climbs through a match, and second-half stoppage time is the most productive window on the field — 1.91× the open-play rate.

Window Goals per live-minute Goals (n)
2nd-half stoppage (the late-game peak) 0.0816 73
1st-half stoppage 0.0478 23
Regulation open play (the floor) 0.0427 675

The reason that peak rate is high isn’t anything about referees — it’s how the football is played late: a losing team throws everyone forward, defenses stretch, the game opens up. Which is exactly the objection in disguise: that rate is endogenous to game state. The minutes I observe at 0.0816 are selected for desperation, so pricing fresh, neutral minutes at that rate would overstate the result. I handle this two ways.

First, the rate decays. I don’t assume the late-game premium persists into the added minutes. Each marginal omitted second-half minute is priced on a curve that decays from the observed 0.0816 back toward the open-play floor of 0.0427, with a half-life I sweep from 2 to 8 minutes (central case: 4). The longer the hypothetical window, the closer each further minute is priced to ordinary open play. The first half keeps its observed rate, since the premium is concentrated in the second. Crucially, the floor is open play — so even under maximum decay, added minutes are never priced below match-average football. They’re still football.

Figure 2. Left: the per-minute rate assigned to an omitted second-half minute, decaying from the observed second-half-stoppage rate (0.0816, dashed) toward the open-play floor (0.0427, dotted), for half-lives of 2, 4 (central), and 8 minutes; the grey histogram is how many omitted minutes matches actually have. Right: the average rate a match effectively receives as a function of its total omitted minutes; the green line marks the typical match (about 6 minutes). Because most matches have modest windows, the effective rate sits well above the floor — but well below the raw peak.

How much does this assumption move the answer? The whole decay band runs from 22.2% (fastest decay) to 24.9% (slowest) — a swing of under three points around the 23.6% headline. The objection is real, it’s priced in, and it moves the result by a couple of points, not by half.

Second, the premium isn’t just a tied-game artifact. If the high rate were purely a product of desperate, level matches, it should collapse when the match isn’t close. It doesn’t. Second-half stoppage scores at about the same rate whether the game is level at 90 minutes (0.0886) or not (0.0786) — the confidence intervals overlap heavily (Figure 3). So conditioning the whole model on score state at 90 minutes barely moves anything: the headline shifts from 23.6% to 23.4%, a fifth of a point.

Figure 3. Second-half-stoppage goal productivity split by the score state at 90 minutes — level, not level, and pooled. The rate is statistically indistinguishable across states (wide, overlapping intervals), which is why conditioning the model on game state moves the headline by only 0.2 points.

Third, this isn’t just an elimination-game artifact. The other natural worry is stakes: maybe the late-game rate is inflated by win-or-go-home knockout matches, where a losing side has no tomorrow and empties the bench forward. It isn’t. Split the same second-half-stoppage window by match type and the rate is statistically flat — group stage 0.0847 (56 goals / 660.8 live-minutes) against elimination 0.0727 (17 goals / 233.7 live-minutes). The confidence intervals overlap heavily, and the point estimate actually leans higher in the group stage — the opposite of the “knockouts are more frantic” intuition (rate ratio 1.17, binomial p = 0.69, not significant; Figure 4). There’s no separate elimination effect to price, so the model keeps a single pooled rate rather than adding a stage term.

Figure 4. Second-half-stoppage goal productivity split by match type — group stage versus elimination. The two rates are statistically indistinguishable (wide, overlapping intervals); the dashed line is the pooled rate. If anything the group stage scores slightly faster, the reverse of the high-stakes-knockout story — which is why the model prices added minutes with one pooled rate and no elimination covariate.

Put together: I grant that late minutes are unusually productive and that the productivity is partly a game-state effect — and after pricing both in, the answer barely moves. The premium is discounted by the decay curve, the part of it that’s a tied-game artifact turns out to be small, and it isn’t a knockout artifact at all.†

† External work points the same direction: post-directive league studies still find referees systematically under-adding stoppage time even after the 2022 crackdown — by a reported ~2 minutes in a 2024 Bundesliga analysis. [Verify and add the exact citation before publishing — this figure is external to my dataset and I have not independently confirmed it.]

8. What this proves, and what it doesn’t

One limitation matters more than the rest, and I’d rather hand it to you than have you find it.

The calibration that anchors the entire model — the owed-stoppage estimator in Step 2 — is fit on World Cup 2018 only. That’s the lone tournament where an independent observer measured the truth by hand, so it’s the only place I can check my work against something outside my own pipeline. Every constant that calibration produces — the restart allowances, the rule for which dead-ball gaps to credit, the small residual correction — is then frozen and applied unchanged to the other five tournaments.

A note on PRE and POST. The six tournaments fall into two eras on either side of one rule-enforcement change. PRE (115 matches) is the two before it — the 2018 World Cup and Euro 2020. POST (199 matches) is the four after — the 2022 World Cup, Euro 2024, Copa América 2024, and AFCON 2023. The dividing line is the directive FIFA issued ahead of the 2022 World Cup: referees’ committee chair Pierluigi Collina told officials to stop under-counting and instead add on all the time genuinely lost, explicitly itemizing goal celebrations, substitutions, injuries, penalties, and VAR delays. The effect was immediate and visible — the typical added-time board jumped from about 7 minutes in the PRE tournaments to 11–12 at the 2022 World Cup. For the model, that boundary is load-bearing in an uncomfortable way: my one calibration tournament, 2018, sits on the PRE side, while the headline mostly lives on the POST side — the side where referees were explicitly told to behave differently. So the estimator is being asked to carry its 2018 assumptions across the exact rule change that defines the two eras.

That’s a real extrapolation, and it’s load-bearing. In the five tournaments without ground truth, estimated owed time runs about 1.5 to 2 times higher than 2018’s (roughly 19 minutes in the POST cohort against 2018’s 12.6; Copa América and AFCON are the highest). And the headline mostly lives in that POST cohort. So the model’s most exposed quantity is precisely the place it can’t be checked: I’m assuming that the way dead time hides inside event data in Copa América and AFCON is structurally similar to how it hides in the 2018 World Cup.

I want to be exact about what the external anchors do and don’t buy. Nate Silver’s 2018 data (r = 0.825) and Opta’s ball-in-play figures for both World Cups (58:04 in 2022, 54:50 in 2018) certify the model’s inputs — the owed-stoppage estimate and the live-time clock. None of them certify the output. There is no independent benchmark anywhere for “what fraction of matches would have ended differently,” so the 23.6% itself is unfalsified against any outside number. It rests on the inputs being right and the 2018-to-everything-else transfer being fair.

I’m stating this plainly rather than burying it because the honest version of this result is: directly validated on the 2018 World Cup, and extended to five other tournaments on the assumption that referees hide owed time in broadly the same way everywhere. If that assumption holds, 23.6% stands. If the post-2022 tournaments behave unlike 2018, the owed time I estimate for them — and therefore the headline — is the first thing that moves. That’s the seam in the work, and it’s where I’d point a skeptic first.

Appendix A. Ball-in-play knob robustness

Appendix Figure A1. Reconstructed ball-in-play minutes for WC2018 and WC2022 across the max_live_gap sweep, against the Opta anchors (54:50 in 2018, 58:04 in 2022). The chosen 20s setting sits near the joint-error minimum; the headline X% moves ≤0.10 pp across the entire sweep (ADR-0026).
